**[10:24](https://youtu.be/NdRRqcvZUjo?t=624)** — So, first we want to make sure that we're actually talking about the same thing. So, am I getting your message? So, the first reason for paraphrasing is understanding. **[11:11](https://youtu.be/NdRRqcvZUjo?t=671)** — different way? Okay. So, there's three different kinds of paraphrases that we're going to um talk about tonight. The first is acknowledging. So, this is just simply um when we're trying to hold we're trying to acknowledge what the person has said by mirroring it back to them. So, again, we're still trying to get the essence of it um but we're just kind of clarifying is this the thing? Then there's an organizing paraphrase where the speaker is trying to help the person prioritize. So this is when somebody comes at you and there's like the, you know, three minute word vomit and you're trying to give them back like so the here's the things that matter to you. It's this, this, and this. Um, and

**[11:56](https://youtu.be/NdRRqcvZUjo?t=716)** — then finally, there's the abstracting paraphrase. Um, and this is what we um this is the research suggests that abstracting paraphrases are the ones that help people shift their thinking the most because what it does is it brings to the surface beliefs, values, um, learning gaps that might need to be addressed. Um, so it surfaces these things that are might be like underneath and like at the root of what's going on. So those are the three different types of paraphrases. And here are a couple. We'll we'll revisit them before you have to practice them. >> But a couple of rules. This is the hardest one. >> Yeah. >> Paraphrases cannot be longer than a sentence. **[12:42](https://youtu.be/NdRRqcvZUjo?t=762)** — compound sentence with lots of ands. >> Dashes, ellipses. You're trying to offer back what's most important. So, if you use the word I, you automatically bring yourself in. So, you're trying to start with you. **[28:35](https://youtu.be/NdRRqcvZUjo?t=1715)** — saying you and we're really just working on trying to get the essence of what they're saying so they feel understood no matter what the messages they're giving us. **[31:49](https://youtu.be/NdRRqcvZUjo?t=1909)** — All right. So, that's the end of our PD demo. Um, but we did also want to just share some um exciting stories from our first year as officially as the Office of Professional Development. Um, so first we just sort of want to give you an overview of like what is it that we're doing. Um, so what we're striving for is to foster a culture of learning across the district where any staff member, we're starting with teachers, but our vision is like that any staff member grows in ways that elevate student achievement and enrich classroom experiences. That's like our ultimate goal. Um, and there's sort of five key areas where we're working right now. Um, the first is in um, instructional leadership development. So we're that's

**[32:34](https://youtu.be/NdRRqcvZUjo?t=1954)** — mostly focusing on our um instructional coaches, curriculum coordinators, and our administrators and really building their skills um as leaders so that they can have an impact on the teachers in their buildings um and then the students. The second one is teacher and staff professional learning. So that focus there is designing and delivering high-quality professional learning um really to strengthen the skills of our staff in the district and ultimately lead to better student outcomes. Uh but we really want to give meaningful learning as well. The third one is to align our curriculum and initiatives across the district so that we can work towards a standards aligned curriculum implementation in all

**[33:20](https://youtu.be/NdRRqcvZUjo?t=2000)** — of our classrooms hopefully and have a uh what we call guaranteed and viable curriculum where we know students are getting similar learning experiences and similar levels of rigor in every classroom. Um the fourth is district coordination and communication. Um so um we want our dream we're working towards it in small steps is that there's like one location where any staff member can find all of the learning experiences to help them grow. um and that that all the communication around it and every department knows like oh like I think that such and such a department has a great um PD that you should attend

**[34:05](https://youtu.be/NdRRqcvZUjo?t=2045)** — because it's going to help you with this skill like let's look and see what next time it's offered. And so of course there's the operational things that happen in any department of creating systems, budgeting that and then also we really focus on collecting data to measure the impact of our of our department and of developing high quality PD in the district because we want to continually improve. We want to model that too, but we want to ensure that what we're doing is having the impact that we hope it does. So over the past year, we've worked very intentionally to build the capacity of our instructional leaders and our

**[34:51](https://youtu.be/NdRRqcvZUjo?t=2091)** — administrators and coaches to deliver highquality professional learning. And we've done this in a couple of ways. We created a set of guidelines that go through many of the most important adult learning standards that help support professional learning for teachers. And we've also created a set of musthaves um because this resource is a pretty in-depth resource and requires a lot of attention and and it takes skill to build that capacity to deliver at that level. So we've started with these musthaves that we think really make a huge impact which is always having clear objectives for any PD that we offer. making sure that there's an agenda with the objectives and learning activities outlined that helps people understand

**[35:38](https://youtu.be/NdRRqcvZUjo?t=2138)** — what's going to happen and feel safe when they walk into that room. We always ask for feedback from participants about the learning experience so we can improve upon it as well as know how to follow up with them. And then we also prioritize engagement using those adult learning principles as we've done today. We've been pretty excited by the feedback that we've gotten from coaches and teachers either in our exit tickets or emails that have been sent to us over the course of this year.
